ITEM 7A. QUANTITATIVE AND QUALITATIVE DISCLOSURES ABOUT MARKET RISK
Financial Instruments and Risk Management
Market Price Sensitive Investments
The fair value of equity securities held by Abbott with a readily determinable fair value was approximately [SALARY] million and [SALARY] million as of [DATE_TIME], respectively. These equity securities are subject to potential changes in fair value. A hypothetical 20 percent decrease in the share prices of these investments would decrease their fair value at [DATE_TIME] by approximately [SALARY] million. Changes in the fair value of these securities are recorded in earnings. The fair value of investments in mutual funds that are held in a rabbi trust for the purpose of paying benefits under a deferred compensation plan was approximately [SALARY] million and [SALARY] million as of [DATE_TIME], respectively. Changes in the fair value of these investments, as well as an offsetting change in the benefit obligation, are recorded in earnings.
Non-Publicly Traded Equity Securities
Abbott holds equity securities that are not traded on public stock exchanges. The carrying value of these investments was [SALARY] million and [SALARY] million as of [DATE_TIME], respectively. No individual investment is recorded at a value in excess of [SALARY] million. Abbott measures these investments at cost minus impairment, if any, plus or minus changes resulting from observable price changes in orderly transactions for the identical or a similar investment of the same issuer.
Interest Rate Sensitive Financial Instruments
At [DATE_TIME], Abbott had interest rate hedge contracts totaling [SALARY].9 billion to manage its exposure to changes in the fair value of debt. The effect of these hedges is to change the fixed interest rate to a variable rate for the portion of the debt that is hedged. Abbott does not use derivative financial instruments, such as interest rate swaps, to manage its exposure to changes in interest rates for its investment securities. The fair value of long-term debt at [DATE_TIME] amounted to [SALARY].8 billion and [SALARY].8 billion, respectively (average interest rates of 3.3% as of [DATE_TIME]) with maturities through 2046. At [DATE_TIME], the fair value of current and long-term investment securities amounted to approximately [SALARY].1 billion and [SALARY].2 billion, respectively. A hypothetical 100-basis point change in the interest rates would not have a material effect on cash flows, income or fair values.
Foreign Currency Sensitive Financial Instruments
Certain Abbott foreign subsidiaries enter into foreign currency forward exchange contracts to manage exposures to changes in foreign exchange rates for anticipated intercompany purchases by those subsidiaries whose functional currencies are not the [LOCATION] dollar. These contracts are designated as cash flow hedges of the variability of the cash flows due to changes in foreign currency exchange rates and are marked-to-market with the resulting gains or losses reflected in Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss). Gains or losses will be included in Cost of products sold at the time the products are sold, generally within [DATE_TIME]. At [DATE_TIME], Abbott held [SALARY].1 billion and [SALARY].8 billion, respectively, of such contracts. Contracts held at [DATE_TIME] will mature in [DATE_TIME] or 2022 depending upon the contract. Contracts held at [DATE_TIME] matured in [DATE_TIME] or will mature in [DATE_TIME] depending upon the contract.
Abbott enters into foreign currency forward exchange contracts to manage its exposure to foreign currency denominated intercompany loans and trade payables and third-party trade payables and receivables. The contracts are marked-to-market, and resulting gains or losses are reflected in income and are generally offset by losses or gains on the foreign currency exposure being managed. At [DATE_TIME], Abbott held [SALARY].0 billion and [SALARY].1 billion, respectively, of such contracts, which mature in [DATE_TIME].
In [DATE_TIME], Abbott borrowed ¥59.8 billion under a [DATE_TIME] term loan and designated the yen-denominated loan as a hedge of the net investment in certain foreign subsidiaries. The proceeds equated to approximately [SALARY] million. The value of this long-term debt was approximately [SALARY] million and [SALARY] million as of [DATE_TIME] and [DATE_TIME], respectively. The change in the value of the debt, which is due to changes in foreign exchange rates, was recorded in Accumulated other comprehensive income (loss), net of tax.
The following table reflects the total foreign currency forward exchange contracts outstanding at [DATE_TIME]:
